            Sort Characters By Frequency (LC 451)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given a string s, sort it in decreasing order based on the frequency of the characters. If two characters have the same frequency, their order doesn't matter. Return the sorted string.

            
  • 1 ≤ s.length ≤ 5 × 105
  • s consists of uppercase/lowercase English letters and digits

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: s = "tree"  |  Output: "eert" (or "eetr")

            
Test Case 2:
Input: s = "cccaaa"  |  Output: "aaaccc" (or "cccaaa")

        

        

            
Frequency Sort Animation (s = "tree")

            

                
 Current

                
 Added to Result

            

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                

                    
Frequency Map

                    

                

                

                    
Max-Heap

                    

                

            

            
Result: ""

        


        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
String frequencySort(String s) {
    // Step 1: Count character frequencies
    Map<Character, Integer> freq = new HashMap<>();
    for (char c : s.toCharArray())
        freq.put(c, freq.getOrDefault(c, 0) + 1);

    // Step 2: Max heap by frequency
    PriorityQueue<Character> maxHeap = new PriorityQueue<>(
        (a, b) -> freq.get(b) - freq.get(a)
    );
    maxHeap.addAll(freq.keySet());

    // Step 3: Build result string
    StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
    while (!maxHeap.isEmpty()) {
        char c = maxHeap.poll();
        for (int i = 0; i < freq.get(c); i++)
            sb.append(c);
    }
    return sb.toString();
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n log n) | Space: O(n)

        

    


    
    

        

            K Closest Points to Origin (LC 973)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given an array of points where points[i] = [xi, yi] represents a point on the X-Y plane and an integer k, return the k closest points to the origin (0, 0). Distance is Euclidean: √(x² + y²).

            
  • 1 ≤ k ≤ points.length ≤ 104

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: points = [[1,3],[-2,2]], k = 1  |  Output: [[-2,2]]

            
Test Case 2:
Input: points = [[3,3],[5,-1],[-2,4]], k = 2
Output: [[3,3],[-2,4]]

        

        

            
K Closest Points Animation (points=[[3,3],[5,-1],[-2,4]], k=2)

            

                
 Current Point

                
 In Heap

                
 Removed (farthest)

            

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                

                    
Points (dist²)

                    

                

                

                    
Max-Heap (by dist)

                    

                

            

            
Heap = [] | Result = ?

        


        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int[][] kClosest(int[][] points, int k) {
    // Max heap by distance (keep k smallest)
    PriorityQueue<int[]> maxHeap = new PriorityQueue<>(
        (a, b) -> (b[0]*b[0] + b[1]*b[1])
                 - (a[0]*a[0] + a[1]*a[1])
    );

    for (int[] p : points) {
        maxHeap.offer(p);
        // If heap exceeds k, remove farthest
        if (maxHeap.size() > k) {
            maxHeap.poll();
        }
    }

    return maxHeap.toArray(new int[0][]);
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n log k) | Space: O(k)

        

    


    
    

        

            Merge K Sorted Lists (LC 23)
            Hard
        

        

            
Problem: Given k sorted linked-lists, merge all into one sorted linked-list and return it.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: lists = [[1,4,5],[1,3,4],[2,6]]
Output: [1,1,2,3,4,4,5,6]

            
Test Case 2:
Input: lists = []  |  Output: []

        


        

            
Merge K Lists Animation (lists=[[1,4,5],[1,3,4],[2,6]])

            

                
 Current Min

                
 In Heap

                
 Merged

            

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                

                    
Lists

                    

                

                

                    
Min-Heap

                    

                

            

            
Merged: []

        


        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
ListNode mergeKLists(ListNode[] lists) {
    if (lists == null || lists.length == 0) return null;
    
    // Min heap by node value
    PriorityQueue minHeap = new PriorityQueue<>(
        (a, b) -> a.val - b.val
    );
    
    // Add first node of each list
    for (ListNode node : lists) {
        if (node != null) minHeap.offer(node);
    }
    
    ListNode dummy = new ListNode(0);
    ListNode current = dummy;
    
    while (!minHeap.isEmpty()) {
        ListNode minNode = minHeap.poll();
        current.next = minNode;
        current = current.next;
        
        // Add next node from same list
        if (minNode.next != null) minHeap.offer(minNode.next);
    }
    
    return dummy.next;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(N log k) | Space: O(k)

        

    


    
    

        

            Kth Smallest Element in M Sorted Arrays
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given M sorted arrays and an integer k, find the kth smallest element among all elements across all arrays. Each array is sorted in ascending order.

            
  • 1 ≤ M ≤ 100
  • 1 ≤ k ≤ total number of elements
  • Each array is sorted in ascending order

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: arrays = [[1,5,9],[2,3,15],[4,8,12]], k = 4
Output: 4 (sorted: 1,2,3,4,5,8,9,12,15)

            
Test Case 2:
Input: arrays = [[10,20],[5,15]], k = 3
Output: 15 (sorted: 5,10,15,20)

        

        

            
Kth Smallest Animation (arrays=[[1,5,9],[2,3,15],[4,8,12]], k=4)

            

                
 Current Min

                
 In Heap

                
 Extracted

            

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                

                    
Arrays

                    

                

                

                    
Min-Heap

                    

                

            

            
count = 0 | Answer = ?

        


        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int kthSmallest(int[][] arrays, int k) {
    // Min heap: {value, arrayIndex, elementIndex}
    PriorityQueue<int[]> minHeap = new PriorityQueue<>(
        (a, b) -> a[0] - b[0]
    );

    // Add first element of each array
    for (int i = 0; i < arrays.length; i++) {
        if (arrays[i].length > 0)
            minHeap.offer(new int[]{arrays[i][0], i, 0});
    }

    int count = 0;
    while (!minHeap.isEmpty()) {
        int[] curr = minHeap.poll();
        count++;
        // If this is the kth element, return it
        if (count == k) return curr[0];

        int arrIdx = curr[1], eleIdx = curr[2];
        // Add next element from same array
        if (eleIdx + 1 < arrays[arrIdx].length) {
            minHeap.offer(new int[]{
                arrays[arrIdx][eleIdx + 1], arrIdx, eleIdx + 1
            });
        }
    }
    return -1;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(k log M) | Space: O(M)

        

    








    

        
8. Binary Trees (27 Questions)

        
Master tree traversals, paths, and structural problems

    


    
    

        

            7. Binary Tree Level Order Traversal (LC 102)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given the root of a binary tree, return the level order traversal (left to right, level by level).

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [3,9,20,null,null,15,7]
Output: [[3],[9,20],[15,7]]

            
Test Case 2:
Input: root = [1]  |  Output: [[1]]

        


        

            
Step-by-Step BFS Animation

            

                
 Current Node

                
 In Queue

                
 Visited

            

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                
3

                
9
20

                
 
 
15
7

            

            
Queue: [] | Result: []

        


        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
List> levelOrder(TreeNode root) {
    List> result = new ArrayList<>();
    if (root == null) return result;
    
    Queue queue = new LinkedList<>();
    queue.offer(root);
    
    while (!queue.isEmpty()) {
        int levelSize = queue.size();
        List level = new ArrayList<>();
        
        for (int i = 0; i < levelSize; i++) {
            TreeNode node = queue.poll();
            level.add(node.val);
            if (node.left != null) queue.offer(node.left);
            if (node.right != null) queue.offer(node.right);
        }
        
        result.add(level);
    }
    
    return result;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(n)

        

    


    
    

        

            Binary Tree Inorder Traversal (LC 94)
            Easy
        

        

            
Problem: Given the root of a binary tree, return the inorder traversal of its nodes' values (Left → Root → Right).

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [1,2,3,4,5]  |  Output: [4,2,5,1,3]

            
Test Case 2:
Input: root = []  |  Output: []

        

        

            
Inorder DFS Animation (root = [1,2,3,4,5])

            

                
 Current

                
 In Stack

                
 Visited

            

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                
1

                
2
3

                
4
5

            

            
result = []

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
List<Integer> result = new ArrayList<>();
void inorder(TreeNode root) {
    if (root == null) return;
    inorder(root.left);
    result.add(root.val);
    inorder(root.right);
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    
    

        

            Binary Tree Preorder Traversal (LC 144)
            Easy
        

        

            
Problem: Given the root of a binary tree, return the preorder traversal of its nodes' values (Root → Left → Right).

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [1,2,3,4,5]  |  Output: [1,2,4,5,3]

            
Test Case 2:
Input: root = [1]  |  Output: [1]

        

        

            
Preorder DFS Animation (root = [1,2,3,4,5])

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                
1

                
2
3

                
4
5

            

            
result = []

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
List<Integer> result = new ArrayList<>();
void preorder(TreeNode root) {
    if (root == null) return;
    result.add(root.val);
    preorder(root.left);
    preorder(root.right);
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    
    

        

            Binary Tree Postorder Traversal (LC 145)
            Easy
        

        

            
Problem: Given the root of a binary tree, return the postorder traversal of its nodes' values (Left → Right → Root).

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [1,2,3,4,5]  |  Output: [4,5,2,3,1]

            
Test Case 2:
Input: root = [1,null,2]  |  Output: [2,1]

        

        

            
Postorder DFS Animation (root = [1,2,3,4,5])

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                
1

                
2
3

                
4
5

            

            
result = []

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
List<Integer> result = new ArrayList<>();
void postorder(TreeNode root) {
    if (root == null) return;
    postorder(root.left);
    postorder(root.right);
    result.add(root.val);
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    
    

        

            Binary Tree Zigzag Level Order Traversal (LC 103)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given the root of a binary tree, return the zigzag level order traversal (alternating left-to-right and right-to-left).

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [3,9,20,null,null,15,7]
Output: [[3],[20,9],[15,7]]

            
Test Case 2:
Input: root = [1]  |  Output: [[1]]

        

        

            
Zigzag BFS Animation

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                
3

                
9
20

                
15
7

            

            
result = []

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
List<List<Integer>> zigzagLevelOrder(TreeNode root) {
    List<List<Integer>> result = new ArrayList<>();
    if (root == null) return result;
    Queue<TreeNode> queue = new LinkedList<>();
    queue.offer(root);
    boolean leftToRight = true;
    while (!queue.isEmpty()) {
        int size = queue.size();
        LinkedList<Integer> level = new LinkedList<>();
        for (int i = 0; i < size; i++) {
            TreeNode node = queue.poll();
            if (leftToRight) level.addLast(node.val);
            else level.addFirst(node.val);
            if (node.left != null) queue.offer(node.left);
            if (node.right != null) queue.offer(node.right);
        }
        result.add(level);
        leftToRight = !leftToRight;
    }
    return result;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(n)

        

    


    
    

        

            Binary Tree Right Side View (LC 199)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given the root of a binary tree, return the values of the nodes you can see from the right side (one node per level).

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [1,2,3,null,5,null,4]
Output: [1,3,4]

            
Test Case 2:
Input: root = [1,2]  |  Output: [1,2]

        

        

            
Right Side View BFS Animation

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                
1

                
2
3

                
 
5
 
4

            

            
result = []

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
List<Integer> rightSideView(TreeNode root) {
    List<Integer> result = new ArrayList<>();
    if (root == null) return result;
    Queue<TreeNode> queue = new LinkedList<>();
    queue.offer(root);
    while (!queue.isEmpty()) {
        int size = queue.size();
        for (int i = 0; i < size; i++) {
            TreeNode node = queue.poll();
            if (i == size - 1) result.add(node.val);
            if (node.left != null) queue.offer(node.left);
            if (node.right != null) queue.offer(node.right);
        }
    }
    return result;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(n)

        

    


    
    

        

            Maximum Depth of Binary Tree (LC 104)
            Easy
        

        

            
Problem: Given the root of a binary tree, return its maximum depth (number of nodes on the longest root-to-leaf path).

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [3,9,20,null,null,15,7]  |  Output: 3

            
Test Case 2:
Input: root = [1,null,2]  |  Output: 2

        

        

            
Max Depth DFS Animation

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                
3

                
9
20

                
15
7

            

            
depth = 0

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int maxDepth(TreeNode root) {
    if (root == null) return 0;
    int left = maxDepth(root.left);
    int right = maxDepth(root.right);
    return 1 + Math.max(left, right);
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    
    

        

            Diameter of Binary Tree (LC 543)
            Easy
        

        

            
Problem: Given the root of a binary tree, return the length of the diameter (longest path between any two nodes, measured by number of edges).

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [1,2,3,4,5]  |  Output: 3 (path 4→2→1→3 or 5→2→1→3)

            
Test Case 2:
Input: root = [1,2]  |  Output: 1

        

        

            
Diameter DFS Animation

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                
1

                
2
3

                
4
5

            

            
diameter = 0

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int diameter = 0;
int diameterOfBinaryTree(TreeNode root) {
    height(root);
    return diameter;
}
int height(TreeNode node) {
    if (node == null) return 0;
    int left = height(node.left);
    int right = height(node.right);
    diameter = Math.max(diameter, left + right);
    return 1 + Math.max(left, right);
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    
    

        

            Balanced Binary Tree (LC 110)
            Easy
        

        

            
Problem: Given a binary tree, determine if it is height-balanced (depth of left and right subtrees differ by at most 1 for every node).

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [3,9,20,null,null,15,7]  |  Output: true

            
Test Case 2:
Input: root = [1,2,2,3,3,null,null,4,4]  |  Output: false

        

        

            
Balanced Check Animation

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                
3

                
9
20

                
15
7

            

            
checking...

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
boolean isBalanced(TreeNode root) {
    return checkHeight(root) != -1;
}
int checkHeight(TreeNode node) {
    if (node == null) return 0;
    int left = checkHeight(node.left);
    if (left == -1) return -1;
    int right = checkHeight(node.right);
    if (right == -1) return -1;
    if (Math.abs(left - right) > 1) return -1;
    return 1 + Math.max(left, right);
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    
    

        

            Same Tree (LC 100)
            Easy
        

        

            
Problem: Given the roots of two binary trees, check if they are structurally identical and have the same node values.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: p = [1,2,3], q = [1,2,3]  |  Output: true

            
Test Case 2:
Input: p = [1,2], q = [1,null,2]  |  Output: false

        

        

            
Same Tree Check Animation

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                

                    

                        
Tree p

                        
1

                        
2
3

                    

                    

                        
Tree q

                        
1

                        
2
3

                    

                

            

            
comparing...

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
boolean isSameTree(TreeNode p, TreeNode q) {
    if (p == null && q == null) return true;
    if (p == null || q == null) return false;
    if (p.val != q.val) return false;
    return isSameTree(p.left, q.left)
        && isSameTree(p.right, q.right);
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    
    

        

            Symmetric Tree (LC 101)
            Easy
        

        

            
Problem: Given the root of a binary tree, check whether it is a mirror of itself (symmetric around its center).

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [1,2,2,3,4,4,3]  |  Output: true

            
Test Case 2:
Input: root = [1,2,2,null,3,null,3]  |  Output: false

        

        

            
Symmetric Check Animation

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                
1

                
2
2

                
3
4
4
3

            

            
checking...

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
boolean isSymmetric(TreeNode root) {
    return isMirror(root.left, root.right);
}
boolean isMirror(TreeNode t1, TreeNode t2) {
    if (t1 == null && t2 == null) return true;
    if (t1 == null || t2 == null) return false;
    return (t1.val == t2.val)
        && isMirror(t1.left, t2.right)
        && isMirror(t1.right, t2.left);
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    
    

        

            Subtree of Another Tree (LC 572)
            Easy
        

        

            
Problem: Given the roots of two binary trees root and subRoot, return true if there is a subtree of root with the same structure and values as subRoot.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [3,4,5,1,2], subRoot = [4,1,2]  |  Output: true

            
Test Case 2:
Input: root = [3,4,5,1,2,null,null,null,null,0], subRoot = [4,1,2]  |  Output: false

        

        

            
Subtree Check Animation

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                

                    

                        
root

                        
3

                        
4
5

                        
1
2

                    

                    

                        
subRoot

                        
4

                        
1
2

                    

                

            

            
checking...

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
boolean isSubtree(TreeNode root, TreeNode subRoot) {
    if (root == null) return false;
    if (isSameTree(root, subRoot)) return true;
    return isSubtree(root.left, subRoot)
        || isSubtree(root.right, subRoot);
}
boolean isSameTree(TreeNode p, TreeNode q) {
    if (p == null && q == null) return true;
    if (p == null || q == null) return false;
    return p.val == q.val
        && isSameTree(p.left, q.left)
        && isSameTree(p.right, q.right);
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(m·n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    
    

        

            Invert Binary Tree (LC 226)
            Easy
        

        

            
Problem: Given the root of a binary tree, invert the tree (swap left and right children at every node) and return its root.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [4,2,7,1,3,6,9]
Output: [4,7,2,9,6,3,1]

            
Test Case 2:
Input: root = [2,1,3]  |  Output: [2,3,1]

        

        

            
Invert Tree Animation

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                
4

                
2
7

                
1
3
6
9

            

            
original tree

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
TreeNode invertTree(TreeNode root) {
    if (root == null) return null;
    TreeNode left = invertTree(root.left);
    TreeNode right = invertTree(root.right);
    root.left = right;
    root.right = left;
    return root;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    
    

        

            Path Sum II (LC 113)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given the root of a binary tree and a target sum, return all root-to-leaf paths where the sum of node values equals the target.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [5,4,8,11,null,13,4,7,2,null,null,5,1], targetSum = 22
Output: [[5,4,11,2],[5,8,4,5]]

            
Test Case 2:
Input: root = [1,2,3], targetSum = 5  |  Output: []

        

        

            
Path Sum II DFS Animation (target=22)

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                
5

                
4
8

                
11
13
4

                
7
2

            

            
path=[] | result=[]

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
List<List<Integer>> pathSum(TreeNode root, int target) {
    List<List<Integer>> result = new ArrayList<>();
    dfs(root, target, new ArrayList<>(), result);
    return result;
}
void dfs(TreeNode node, int remain, List<Integer> path, List<List<Integer>> result) {
    if (node == null) return;
    path.add(node.val);
    if (node.left == null && node.right == null && remain == node.val)
        result.add(new ArrayList<>(path));
    dfs(node.left, remain - node.val, path, result);
    dfs(node.right, remain - node.val, path, result);
    path.remove(path.size() - 1);
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n²) | Space: O(n)

        

    


    
    

        

            Path Sum III (LC 437)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given the root of a binary tree and a target sum, return the number of paths where values sum to target. The path does not need to start at root or end at leaf.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [10,5,-3,3,2,null,11,3,-2,null,1], targetSum = 8
Output: 3

            
Test Case 2:
Input: root = [1,2], targetSum = 3  |  Output: 1

        

        

            
Path Sum III Prefix Sum Animation

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                
10

                
5
-3

                
3
2
11

            

            
count=0 | prefixSum=0

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int pathSum(TreeNode root, int targetSum) {
    Map<Long, Integer> map = new HashMap<>();
    map.put(0L, 1);
    return dfs(root, 0, targetSum, map);
}
int dfs(TreeNode node, long curSum, int target, Map<Long, Integer> map) {
    if (node == null) return 0;
    curSum += node.val;
    int count = map.getOrDefault(curSum - target, 0);
    map.put(curSum, map.getOrDefault(curSum, 0) + 1);
    count += dfs(node.left, curSum, target, map);
    count += dfs(node.right, curSum, target, map);
    map.put(curSum, map.get(curSum) - 1);
    return count;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(n)

        

    


    
    

        

            Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum (LC 124)
            Hard
        

        

            
Problem: Given the root of a binary tree, return the maximum path sum. A path is any sequence of nodes connected by edges (doesn't need to pass through root).

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [-10,9,20,null,null,15,7]  |  Output: 42 (15→20→7)

            
Test Case 2:
Input: root = [1,2,3]  |  Output: 6 (2→1→3)

        

        

            
Max Path Sum DFS Animation

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                
-10

                
9
20

                
15
7

            

            
maxSum = -∞

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int maxSum = Integer.MIN_VALUE;
int maxPathSum(TreeNode root) {
    gain(root);
    return maxSum;
}
int gain(TreeNode node) {
    if (node == null) return 0;
    int left = Math.max(gain(node.left), 0);
    int right = Math.max(gain(node.right), 0);
    maxSum = Math.max(maxSum, node.val + left + right);
    return node.val + Math.max(left, right);
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    
    

        

            Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Tree (LC 236)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given a binary tree, find the lowest common ancestor (LCA) of two given nodes p and q.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [3,5,1,6,2,0,8], p=5, q=1  |  Output: 3

            
Test Case 2:
Input: root = [3,5,1,6,2,0,8], p=5, q=4  |  Output: 5

        

        

            
LCA DFS Animation (p=5, q=1)

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                
3

                
5
1

                
6
2
0
8

            

            
searching...

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
TreeNode lowestCommonAncestor(TreeNode root, TreeNode p, TreeNode q) {
    if (root == null) return null;
    if (root == p || root == q) return root;
    TreeNode left = lowestCommonAncestor(root.left, p, q);
    TreeNode right = lowestCommonAncestor(root.right, p, q);
    if (left != null && right != null) return root;
    return left != null ? left : right;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    
    

        

            Lowest Common Ancestor of a BST (LC 235)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given a BST, find the lowest common ancestor of two given nodes p and q. Use BST property for O(h) efficiency.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [6,2,8,0,4,7,9], p=2, q=8  |  Output: 6

            
Test Case 2:
Input: root = [6,2,8,0,4,7,9], p=2, q=4  |  Output: 2

        

        

            
LCA BST Animation (p=2, q=8)

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                
6

                
2
8

                
0
4
7
9

            

            
searching...

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
TreeNode lowestCommonAncestor(TreeNode root, TreeNode p, TreeNode q) {
    while (root != null) {
        if (p.val < root.val && q.val < root.val)
            root = root.left;
        else if (p.val > root.val && q.val > root.val)
            root = root.right;
        else
            return root;
    }
    return null;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(h) | Space: O(1)

        

    


    
    

        

            Validate Binary Search Tree (LC 98)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given the root of a binary tree, determine if it is a valid BST (left subtree values < node < right subtree values).

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [2,1,3]  |  Output: true

            
Test Case 2:
Input: root = [5,1,4,null,null,3,6]  |  Output: false (4 has child 3 < 5)

        

        

            
Validate BST Animation (root = [5,1,4,null,null,3,6])

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                
5

                
1
4

                
3
6

            

            
checking bounds...

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
boolean isValidBST(TreeNode root) {
    return validate(root, Long.MIN_VALUE, Long.MAX_VALUE);
}
boolean validate(TreeNode node, long min, long max) {
    if (node == null) return true;
    if (node.val <= min || node.val >= max) return false;
    return validate(node.left, min, node.val)
        && validate(node.right, node.val, max);
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    
    

        

            Kth Smallest Element in a BST (LC 230)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given the root of a BST and an integer k, return the kth smallest value in the tree.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [3,1,4,null,2], k = 1  |  Output: 1

            
Test Case 2:
Input: root = [5,3,6,2,4,null,null,1], k = 3  |  Output: 3

        

        

            
Kth Smallest Inorder Animation (k=1)

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                
3

                
1
4

                
2

            

            
count=0 | k=1

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int count = 0, result = 0;
int kthSmallest(TreeNode root, int k) {
    inorder(root, k);
    return result;
}
void inorder(TreeNode node, int k) {
    if (node == null) return;
    inorder(node.left, k);
    count++;
    if (count == k) { result = node.val; return; }
    inorder(node.right, k);
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(h + k) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    
    

        

            Search in a Binary Search Tree (LC 700)
            Easy
        

        

            
Problem: Given the root of a BST and a value, find the node whose value equals val and return the subtree rooted at that node.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [4,2,7,1,3], val = 2  |  Output: [2,1,3]

            
Test Case 2:
Input: root = [4,2,7,1,3], val = 5  |  Output: null

        

        

            
Search BST Animation (val=2)

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                
4

                
2
7

                
1
3

            

            
searching for 2...

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
TreeNode searchBST(TreeNode root, int val) {
    if (root == null) return null;
    if (val == root.val) return root;
    if (val < root.val) return searchBST(root.left, val);
    return searchBST(root.right, val);
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(h) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    
    

        

            Insert into a Binary Search Tree (LC 701)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given the root of a BST and a value to insert, insert the value into the BST and return the root.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [4,2,7,1,3], val = 5  |  Output: [4,2,7,1,3,5]

            
Test Case 2:
Input: root = [40,20,60,10,30], val = 25  |  Output: [40,20,60,10,30,null,null,null,null,25]

        

        

            
Insert BST Animation (val=5)

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                
4

                
2
7

                
1
3
5

            

            
inserting 5...

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
TreeNode insertIntoBST(TreeNode root, int val) {
    if (root == null) return new TreeNode(val);
    if (val < root.val)
        root.left = insertIntoBST(root.left, val);
    else
        root.right = insertIntoBST(root.right, val);
    return root;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(h) | Space: O(h)

        

    


    
    

        

            Convert Sorted Array to Binary Search Tree (LC 108)
            Easy
        

        

            
Problem: Given an integer array sorted in ascending order, convert it to a height-balanced BST.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: nums = [-10,-3,0,5,9]  |  Output: [0,-3,9,-10,null,5]

            
Test Case 2:
Input: nums = [1,3]  |  Output: [3,1] or [1,null,3]

        

        

            
Sorted Array to BST Animation

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

            

                
 

                
 
 

                
 
 

            

            
building BST...

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
TreeNode sortedArrayToBST(int[] nums) {
    return build(nums, 0, nums.length - 1);
}
TreeNode build(int[] nums, int left, int right) {
    if (left > right) return null;
    int mid = left + (right - left) / 2;
    TreeNode node = new TreeNode(nums[mid]);
    node.left = build(nums, left, mid - 1);
    node.right = build(nums, mid + 1, right);
    return node;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(log n)

        

    


    
    

        

            Construct Binary Tree from Preorder and Inorder (LC 105)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given two integer arrays preorder and inorder, construct and return the binary tree.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: preorder = [3,9,20,15,7], inorder = [9,3,15,20,7]
Output: [3,9,20,null,null,15,7]

            
Test Case 2:
Input: preorder = [-1], inorder = [-1]  |  Output: [-1]

        

        

            
Construct from Pre+In Animation

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

            

                
 

                
 
 

                
 
 

            

            
building...

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int preIdx = 0;
Map<Integer,Integer> inMap = new HashMap<>();
TreeNode buildTree(int[] preorder, int[] inorder) {
    for (int i = 0; i < inorder.length; i++) inMap.put(inorder[i], i);
    return build(preorder, 0, inorder.length - 1);
}
TreeNode build(int[] preorder, int inLeft, int inRight) {
    if (inLeft > inRight) return null;
    int rootVal = preorder[preIdx++];
    TreeNode root = new TreeNode(rootVal);
    int inIdx = inMap.get(rootVal);
    root.left = build(preorder, inLeft, inIdx - 1);
    root.right = build(preorder, inIdx + 1, inRight);
    return root;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(n)

        

    


    
    

        

            Construct Binary Tree from Inorder and Postorder (LC 106)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given two integer arrays inorder and postorder, construct and return the binary tree.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: inorder = [9,3,15,20,7], postorder = [9,15,7,20,3]
Output: [3,9,20,null,null,15,7]

            
Test Case 2:
Input: inorder = [-1], postorder = [-1]  |  Output: [-1]

        

        

            
Construct from In+Post Animation

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

            

                
 

                
 
 

                
 
 

            

            
building...

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int postIdx;
Map<Integer,Integer> inMap = new HashMap<>();
TreeNode buildTree(int[] inorder, int[] postorder) {
    postIdx = postorder.length - 1;
    for (int i = 0; i < inorder.length; i++) inMap.put(inorder[i], i);
    return build(postorder, 0, inorder.length - 1);
}
TreeNode build(int[] postorder, int inLeft, int inRight) {
    if (inLeft > inRight) return null;
    int rootVal = postorder[postIdx--];
    TreeNode root = new TreeNode(rootVal);
    int inIdx = inMap.get(rootVal);
    root.right = build(postorder, inIdx + 1, inRight);
    root.left = build(postorder, inLeft, inIdx - 1);
    return root;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(n)

        

    


    
    

        

            Serialize and Deserialize Binary Tree (LC 297)
            Hard
        

        

            
Problem: Design an algorithm to serialize a binary tree to a string and deserialize the string back to a tree.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [1,2,3,null,null,4,5]
Serialized: "1,2,null,null,3,4,null,null,5,null,null"

            
Test Case 2:
Input: root = []  |  Serialized: "null"

        

        

            
Serialize/Deserialize Animation

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                
1

                
2
3

                
4
5

            

            
serialized = ""

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
String serialize(TreeNode root) {
    if (root == null) return "null";
    return root.val + "," + serialize(root.left) + "," + serialize(root.right);
}
TreeNode deserialize(String data) {
    Queue<String> queue = new LinkedList<>(Arrays.asList(data.split(",")));
    return buildTree(queue);
}
TreeNode buildTree(Queue<String> q) {
    String val = q.poll();
    if (val.equals("null")) return null;
    TreeNode node = new TreeNode(Integer.parseInt(val));
    node.left = buildTree(q);
    node.right = buildTree(q);
    return node;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(n)

        

    


    
    

        

            Flatten Binary Tree to Linked List (LC 114)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given the root of a binary tree, flatten it into a linked list in-place using preorder traversal (all left pointers become null, right pointers chain nodes).

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: root = [1,2,5,3,4,null,6]
Output: [1,null,2,null,3,null,4,null,5,null,6]

            
Test Case 2:
Input: root = []  |  Output: []

        

        

            
Flatten Tree Animation

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                
1

                
2
5

                
3
4
6

            

            
flattening...

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
TreeNode prev = null;
void flatten(TreeNode root) {
    if (root == null) return;
    flatten(root.right);
    flatten(root.left);
    root.right = prev;
    root.left = null;
    prev = root;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(h)

        

    









    

        
9. Dynamic Programming (25 Questions)

        
Solve optimization problems using memoization and tabulation

    


    
    

        

            8. Climbing Stairs (LC 70)
            Easy
        

        

            
Problem: You can climb 1 or 2 steps at a time. How many distinct ways can you climb n steps?

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: n = 2  |  Output: 2  (1+1 or 2)

            
Test Case 2:
Input: n = 5  |  Output: 8

        


        

            
DP Table Filling Animation (n = 5)

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                
Step012345

                
dp[i]------

            

            

                
Formula: dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]

                

            

            
Answer: ?

        


        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int climbStairs(int n) {
    if (n <= 2) return n;
    
    int prev2 = 1; // dp[i-2]
    int prev1 = 2; // dp[i-1]
    
    for (int i = 3; i <= n; i++) {
        int current = prev1 + prev2;
        prev2 = prev1;
        prev1 = current;
    }
    
    return prev1;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(1)

        

    


    
    

        

            9. Coin Change (LC 322)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given coin denominations and an amount, return the fewest coins needed. Return -1 if impossible.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: coins = [1,2,5], amount = 11  |  Output: 3  (5+5+1)

            
Test Case 2:
Input: coins = [2], amount = 3        |  Output: -1

        


        

            
DP Table Filling Animation (coins=[1,2,5], amount=7)

            

                
 Current Cell

                
 Comparing

                
 Filled

            

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                
Amount01234567

                
dp--------

            

            
Coins: [1, 2, 5] | Answer: ?

        


        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int coinChange(int[] coins, int amount) {
    int[] dp = new int[amount + 1];
    Arrays.fill(dp, amount + 1);
    dp[0] = 0;
    
    for (int i = 1; i <= amount; i++) {
        for (int coin : coins) {
            if (coin <= i) {
                dp[i] = Math.min(dp[i], dp[i - coin] + 1);
            }
        }
    }
    
    return dp[amount] > amount ? -1 : dp[amount];
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(amount * n) | Space: O(amount)

        

    


    
    

        

            10. Longest Common Subsequence (LC 1143)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given two strings, return the length of their longest common subsequence.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: text1 = "abcde", text2 = "ace"  |  Output: 3 ("ace")

            
Test Case 2:
Input: text1 = "abc", text2 = "def"    |  Output: 0

        


        

            
2D DP Table Animation (text1="ace", text2="abcde")

            

                
 Current Cell

                
 Comparing

                
 Match

            

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

            
LCS Length: ?

        


        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int longestCommonSubsequence(String text1, String text2) {
    int m = text1.length(), n = text2.length();
    int[][] dp = new int[m + 1][n + 1];
    
    for (int i = 1; i <= m; i++) {
        for (int j = 1; j <= n; j++) {
            if (text1.charAt(i-1) == text2.charAt(j-1)) {
                // Characters match
                dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1;
            } else {
                dp[i][j] = Math.max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]);
            }
        }
    }
    
    return dp[m][n];
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(m*n) | Space: O(m*n)

        

    


    
    

        

            11. House Robber (LC 198)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: You are a robber planning to rob houses along a street. Each house has a non-negative amount of money. Adjacent houses have security connected — you cannot rob two adjacent houses. Return the maximum money you can rob tonight.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: nums = [2,7,9,3,1]  |  Output: 12  (rob 2 + 9 + 1)

            
Test Case 2:
Input: nums = [1,2,3,1]     |  Output: 4   (rob 1 + 3)

        

        

            
1D DP Table (nums = [2,7,9,3,1])

            

                
 Current index

                
 Filled

            

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                
i01234

                
nums27931

                
dp-----

            

            
Answer: ?

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int rob(int[] nums) {
    int n = nums.length;
    if (n == 1) return nums[0];
    int[] dp = new int[n];
    dp[0] = nums[0];
    dp[1] = Math.max(nums[0], nums[1]);
    for (int i = 2; i < n; i++) {
        dp[i] = Math.max(dp[i-1], nums[i] + dp[i-2]);
    }
    return dp[n - 1];
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(n)

        

    


    
    

        

            12. House Robber II (LC 213)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Same as House Robber, but houses are arranged in a circle: the first and last houses are neighbors. You still cannot rob two adjacent houses. Return the maximum money you can rob.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: nums = [2,3,2]  |  Output: 3  (rob house 1 only; cannot rob 0 and 2 together)

            
Test Case 2:
Input: nums = [1,2,3,1]  |  Output: 4

        

        

            
Two linear ranges (nums = [2,3,2]) — exclude last vs exclude first

            

                
 Active range

                
 prev2 / prev1

            

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            
Range: —

            

                
i012

                
nums232

                
take———

            

            
prev2 = 0, prev1 = 0

            
Answer: ?

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int rob(int[] nums) {
    int n = nums.length;
    if (n == 1) return nums[0];
    int a = robRange(nums, 0, n - 2);
    int b = robRange(nums, 1, n - 1);
    return Math.max(a, b);
}
int robRange(int[] nums, int lo, int hi) {
    int prev2 = 0;
    int prev1 = 0;
    for (int i = lo; i <= hi; i++) {
        int cur = Math.max(prev1, nums[i] + prev2);
        prev2 = prev1;
        prev1 = cur;
    }
    return prev1;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(1)

        

    


    
    

        

            13. Min Cost Climbing Stairs (LC 746)
            Easy
        

        

            
Problem: Each index of an integer array cost is a stair; cost[i] is the cost to step on it. You may start at index 0 or 1. Each move goes 1 or 2 stairs up. Return the minimum cost to reach the top (past the last index).

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: cost = [10,15,20]  |  Output: 15  (start at 1, pay 15, jump to top)

            
Test Case 2:
Input: cost = [1,100,1,1,1,100,1,1,100,1]  |  Output: 6

        

        

            
dp[i] = min cost to reach step i (cost = [10,15,20])

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

                
step0123 (top)

                
dp----

            

            
cost[0]=10, cost[1]=15, cost[2]=20 — indices 0,1,2 are stairs; step 3 is the top.

            
Answer: ?

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int minCostClimbingStairs(int[] cost) {
    int n = cost.length;
    int[] dp = new int[n + 1];
    dp[0] = 0;
    dp[1] = 0;
    for (int i = 2; i <= n; i++) {
        dp[i] = Math.min(dp[i - 1] + cost[i - 1], dp[i - 2] + cost[i - 2]);
    }
    return dp[n];
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n) | Space: O(n)

        

    


    
    

        

            14. Unique Paths (LC 62)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: A robot starts at the top-left corner of an m x n grid and tries to reach the bottom-right. It can only move right or down. How many unique paths are there?

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: m = 3, n = 3  |  Output: 6

            
Test Case 2:
Input: m = 3, n = 7  |  Output: 28

        

        

            
Grid DP (m = 3, n = 3)

            

                
 Current cell

                
 Filled

            

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

            
Answer: ?

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int uniquePaths(int m, int n) {
    int[][] dp = new int[m][n];
    for (int j = 0; j < n; j++) {
        dp[0][j] = 1;
    }
    for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) {
        dp[i][0] = 1;
    }
    for (int i = 1; i < m; i++) {
        for (int j = 1; j < n; j++) {
            dp[i][j] = dp[i - 1][j] + dp[i][j - 1];
        }
    }
    return dp[m - 1][n - 1];
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(m * n) | Space: O(m * n)

        

    


    
    

        

            15. Unique Paths II (LC 63)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Same as Unique Paths, but the grid may contain obstacles marked as 1 (blocked). Empty cells are 0. An obstacle has zero paths through it.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: grid = [[0,0,0],[0,1,0],[0,0,0]]  |  Output: 2

            
Test Case 2:
Input: grid = [[0,1],[0,0]]  |  Output: 1

        

        

            
Grid with obstacle at [1][1]

            

                
 Obstacle

                
 Current

            

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

            
Answer: ?

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int uniquePathsWithObstacles(int[][] grid) {
    int m = grid.length;
    int n = grid[0].length;
    if (grid[0][0] == 1) return 0;
    int[][] dp = new int[m][n];
    dp[0][0] = 1;
    for (int i = 1; i < m; i++) {
        dp[i][0] = grid[i][0] == 1 ? 0 : dp[i - 1][0];
    }
    for (int j = 1; j < n; j++) {
        dp[0][j] = grid[0][j] == 1 ? 0 : dp[0][j - 1];
    }
    for (int i = 1; i < m; i++) {
        for (int j = 1; j < n; j++) {
            if (grid[i][j] == 1) {
                dp[i][j] = 0;
            } else {
                dp[i][j] = dp[i - 1][j] + dp[i][j - 1];
            }
        }
    }
    return dp[m - 1][n - 1];
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(m * n) | Space: O(m * n)

        

    


    
    

        

            16. Minimum Path Sum (LC 64)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given an m x n grid of non-negative numbers, find a path from top-left to bottom-right that minimizes the sum of values along the path. You may only move right or down.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: grid = [[1,3,1],[1,5,1],[4,2,1]]  |  Output: 7  (1→1→3→1→1)

            
Test Case 2:
Input: grid = [[1,2,3],[4,5,6]]  |  Output: 12

        

        

            
Minimum sum path DP

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

            
Values show min path sum to each cell.

            

            
Answer: ?

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int minPathSum(int[][] grid) {
    int m = grid.length;
    int n = grid[0].length;
    int[][] dp = new int[m][n];
    dp[0][0] = grid[0][0];
    for (int i = 1; i < m; i++) {
        dp[i][0] = dp[i - 1][0] + grid[i][0];
    }
    for (int j = 1; j < n; j++) {
        dp[0][j] = dp[0][j - 1] + grid[0][j];
    }
    for (int i = 1; i < m; i++) {
        for (int j = 1; j < n; j++) {
            dp[i][j] = Math.min(dp[i - 1][j], dp[i][j - 1]) + grid[i][j];
        }
    }
    return dp[m - 1][n - 1];
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(m * n) | Space: O(m * n)

        

    


    
    

        

            17. Maximal Square (LC 221)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given an m x n binary matrix filled with 0 and 1, find the largest square containing only 1s and return its area (side length squared).

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: matrix = [["1","0","1"],["1","1","1"],["0","1","1"]]  |  Output: 4

            
Test Case 2:
Input: matrix = [["0","1"],["1","0"]]  |  Output: 1

        

        

            
dp[i][j] = side length of largest square of 1s ending at (i,j)

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

            

            
Answer (area): ?

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int maximalSquare(char[][] matrix) {
    int m = matrix.length;
    int n = matrix[0].length;
    int[][] dp = new int[m][n];
    int best = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) {
        for (int j = 0; j < n; j++) {
            if (matrix[i][j] == '1') {
                if (i == 0 || j == 0) {
                    dp[i][j] = 1;
                } else {
                    int up = dp[i - 1][j];
                    int left = dp[i][j - 1];
                    int diag = dp[i - 1][j - 1];
                    dp[i][j] = Math.min(Math.min(up, left), diag) + 1;
                }
                best = Math.max(best, dp[i][j]);
            } else {
                dp[i][j] = 0;
            }
        }
    }
    return best * best;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(m * n) | Space: O(m * n)

        

    


    
    

        

            18. Partition Equal Subset Sum (LC 416)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given a non-empty array of positive integers, determine if it can be partitioned into two subsets with equal sum.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: nums = [1,5,11,5]  |  Output: true  (subset sums 11 and 11)

            
Test Case 2:
Input: nums = [1,2,3,5]    |  Output: false

        

        

            
Subset-sum DP (nums = [1,5,11,5], target = 11)

            

                
 Current j

                
 Reachable sum

            

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            
num: —

            

                
sum j
                    01234567891011

                
dp[j]
                    ------------

            

            
Answer: ?

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
boolean canPartition(int[] nums) {
    int sum = 0;
    for (int x : nums) sum += x;
    if (sum % 2 != 0) return false;
    int target = sum / 2;
    boolean[] dp = new boolean[target + 1];
    dp[0] = true;
    for (int num : nums) {
        for (int j = target; j >= num; j--) {
            dp[j] = dp[j] || dp[j - num];
        }
    }
    return dp[target];
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n * target) | Space: O(target)

        

    


    
    

        

            19. Target Sum (LC 494)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Assign + or - before each integer in nums so the expression equals target. Return the number of different expressions that work.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: nums = [1,1,1,1,1], target = 3  |  Output: 5

            
Test Case 2:
Input: nums = [1], target = 1             |  Output: 1

        

        

            
Count subsets with sum (sum+target)/2 — t = 4

            

                
 Current j

                
 Ways

            

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            
num: —

            

                
j01234

                
dp[j]
                    -----

            

            
Answer: ?

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int findTargetSumWays(int[] nums, int target) {
    int sum = 0;
    for (int x : nums) sum += x;
    if (target > sum || target < -sum) return 0;
    if ((sum + target) % 2 != 0) return 0;
    int t = (sum + target) / 2;
    int[] dp = new int[t + 1];
    dp[0] = 1;
    for (int num : nums) {
        for (int j = t; j >= num; j--) {
            dp[j] += dp[j - num];
        }
    }
    return dp[t];
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n * t) | Space: O(t)

        

    


    
    

        

            20. Last Stone Weight II (LC 1049)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: On each turn, pick two stones and smash them: if equal, both destroyed; else the larger breaks into |a-b|. Return the smallest possible weight of the last remaining stone (same as minimizing pile difference — knapsack to sum/2).

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: stones = [2,7,4,1,8,1]  |  Output: 1

            
Test Case 2:
Input: stones = [31,26,33,21,40]  |  Output: 5

        

        

            
Max subset ≤ sum/2 (stones = [2,7,4,1,8,1], sum=23, target=11)

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            
weight: —

            

                
j
                    01234567891011

                
dp[j]
                    ------------

            

            
Answer: ?

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int lastStoneWeightII(int[] stones) {
    int sum = 0;
    for (int w : stones) sum += w;
    int target = sum / 2;
    boolean[] dp = new boolean[target + 1];
    dp[0] = true;
    for (int w : stones) {
        for (int j = target; j >= w; j--) {
            dp[j] = dp[j] || dp[j - w];
        }
    }
    int best = 0;
    for (int j = target; j >= 0; j--) {
        if (dp[j]) { best = j; break; }
    }
    return sum - 2 * best;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n * target) | Space: O(target)

        

    


    
    

        

            21. Coin Change II (LC 518)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: You have an integer array coins of distinct coin values and an integer amount. Return the number of combinations that make up that amount (order of coins does not matter; unlimited supply of each coin).

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: amount = 5, coins = [1,2,5]  |  Output: 4

            
Test Case 2:
Input: amount = 3, coins = [2]       |  Output: 0

        

        

            
Unbounded knapsack count (amount = 5, coins = [1,2,5])

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            
coin: —

            

                
j012345

                
dp[j]
                    ------

            

            
Answer: ?

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int change(int amount, int[] coins) {
    int[] dp = new int[amount + 1];
    dp[0] = 1;
    for (int coin : coins) {
        for (int j = coin; j <= amount; j++) {
            dp[j] += dp[j - coin];
        }
    }
    return dp[amount];
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(amount * coins.length) | Space: O(amount)

        

    


    
    

        

            22. Perfect Squares (LC 279)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given an integer n, return the least number of perfect square numbers (e.g. 1, 4, 9, …) that sum to n.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: n = 12  |  Output: 3  (4 + 4 + 4)

            
Test Case 2:
Input: n = 13  |  Output: 2  (4 + 9)

        

        

            
dp[i] = min squares summing to i (n = 12)

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            
i: —

            

                
i
                    0123456789101112

                
dp
                    -------------

            

            
Answer: ?

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int numSquares(int n) {
    int[] dp = new int[n + 1];
    for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
        dp[i] = i;
        for (int j = 1; j * j <= i; j++) {
            int sq = j * j;
            dp[i] = Math.min(dp[i], dp[i - sq] + 1);
        }
    }
    return dp[n];
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n * sqrt(n)) | Space: O(n)

        

    


    
    

        

            23. Edit Distance (LC 72)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given two strings, return the minimum number of operations to convert word1 into word2. You may insert, delete, or replace a character; each costs 1.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: word1 = "horse", word2 = "ros"  |  Output: 3

            
Test Case 2:
Input: word1 = "intention", word2 = "execution"  |  Output: 5

        

        

            
2D DP (word1 = "horse", word2 = "ros")

            

                
 Current cell

                
 Compared neighbors

            

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

            
Answer: ?

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int minDistance(String a, String b) {
    int m = a.length();
    int n = b.length();
    int[][] dp = new int[m + 1][n + 1];
    for (int i = 0; i <= m; i++) {
        dp[i][0] = i;
    }
    for (int j = 0; j <= n; j++) {
        dp[0][j] = j;
    }
    for (int i = 1; i <= m; i++) {
        for (int j = 1; j <= n; j++) {
            if (a.charAt(i - 1) == b.charAt(j - 1)) {
                dp[i][j] = dp[i - 1][j - 1];
            } else {
                int del = dp[i - 1][j] + 1;
                int ins = dp[i][j - 1] + 1;
                int rep = dp[i - 1][j - 1] + 1;
                dp[i][j] = Math.min(Math.min(del, ins), rep);
            }
        }
    }
    return dp[m][n];
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(m * n) | Space: O(m * n)

        

    


    
    

        

            24. Delete Operation for Two Strings (LC 583)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given two strings, return the minimum sum of steps (each step deletes exactly one character in either string) so the two strings become equal.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: word1 = "sea", word2 = "eat"  |  Output: 2

            
Test Case 2:
Input: word1 = "leetcode", word2 = "etco"  |  Output: 4

        

        

            
Min deletions DP (word1 = "sea", word2 = "eat")

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

            
Answer: ?

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int minDistance(String w1, String w2) {
    int m = w1.length();
    int n = w2.length();
    int[][] dp = new int[m + 1][n + 1];
    for (int i = 0; i <= m; i++) {
        for (int j = 0; j <= n; j++) {
            if (i == 0 || j == 0) {
                dp[i][j] = i + j;
            } else if (w1.charAt(i - 1) == w2.charAt(j - 1)) {
                dp[i][j] = dp[i - 1][j - 1];
            } else {
                dp[i][j] = 1 + Math.min(dp[i - 1][j], dp[i][j - 1]);
            }
        }
    }
    return dp[m][n];
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(m * n) | Space: O(m * n)

        

    


    
    

        

            25. Longest Increasing Subsequence (LC 300)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given an integer array nums, return the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: nums = [10,9,2,5,3,7,101,18]  |  Output: 4  (e.g. 2,5,7,101)

            
Test Case 2:
Input: nums = [0,1,0,3,2,3]           |  Output: 4

        

        

            
O(n²) DP — dp[i] = LIS ending at i

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            
nums: 10, 9, 2, 5, 3, 7, 101, 18

            

                
idx01234567

                
dp
                    --------

            

            
Answer: ?

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int lengthOfLIS(int[] nums) {
    int n = nums.length;
    int[] dp = new int[n];
    int best = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        dp[i] = 1;
        for (int j = 0; j < i; j++) {
            if (nums[j] < nums[i]) {
                dp[i] = Math.max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1);
            }
        }
        best = Math.max(best, dp[i]);
    }
    return best;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n²) | Space: O(n)

        

    


    
    

        

            26. Russian Doll Envelopes (LC 354)
            Hard
        

        

            
Problem: You have a number of envelopes with widths and heights. One envelope fits inside another only if both dimensions are strictly larger. Return the maximum number of envelopes you can Russian doll (nest).

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: envelopes = [[5,4],[6,4],[6,7],[2,3]]  |  Output: 3

            
Test Case 2:
Input: envelopes = [[1,1],[1,1],[1,1]]  |  Output: 1

        

        

            
Sort by width ↑, height ↓, then LIS on heights (patience / binary search)

            

                
 Current height / tails cell

                
 tails array

            

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            
Sorted: —

            

                
idx0123

                
(w,h)
                    ----

                
h
                    ----

            

            
tails (smallest tail for each LIS length)

            

                
i012345

                
val
                    ——————

            

            
Answer: ?

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int maxEnvelopes(int[][] env) {
    Arrays.sort(env, (a, b) -> a[0] != b[0] ? a[0] - b[0] : b[1] - a[1]);
    int n = env.length;
    int[] heights = new int[n];
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) heights[i] = env[i][1];
    List tails = new ArrayList<>();
    for (int k = 0; k < n; k++) {
        int x = heights[k];
        int lo = 0, hi = tails.size();
        while (lo < hi) {
            int mid = (lo + hi) / 2;
            if (tails.get(mid) < x) lo = mid + 1;
            else hi = mid;
        }
        if (lo == tails.size()) tails.add(x);
        else tails.set(lo, x);
    }
    return tails.size();
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n log n) | Space: O(n)

        

    


    
    

        

            27. Longest String Chain (LC 1048)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given an array of unique strings, a chain is a sequence where each word can be formed by adding exactly one letter to the previous word. Return the length of the longest possible chain.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: words = ["a","b","ba","bca","bda","bdca"]  |  Output: 4

            
Test Case 2:
Input: words = ["xbc","pcxbcf","xb","cxbc","pcxbc"]  |  Output: 5

        

        

            
Sort by length, then dp[word] = 1 + max(dp[pred]) over predecessors

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            
word: —

            

                
idx012345

                
word
                    ------

                
dp
                    ------

            

            
Answer: ?

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int longestStrChain(String[] words) {
    Arrays.sort(words, (a, b) -> a.length() - b.length());
    Map dp = new HashMap<>();
    int best = 0;
    for (String w : words) {
        int cur = 1;
        for (int i = 0; i < w.length(); i++) {
            String pred = w.substring(0, i) + w.substring(i + 1);
            cur = Math.max(cur, dp.getOrDefault(pred, 0) + 1);
        }
        dp.put(w, cur);
        best = Math.max(best, cur);
    }
    return best;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n * L²) with word lengths L | Space: O(n * L)

        

    


    
    

        

            28. Longest Palindromic Substring (LC 5)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given a string s, return the longest palindromic substring in s.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: s = "babad"  |  Output: "bab" (or "aba")

            
Test Case 2:
Input: s = "cbbd"  |  Output: "bb"

        

        

            
2D DP: dp[i][j] = true if s[i..j] is palindrome (s = "babad")

            

                
 Current cell

            

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

            
Answer: ?

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
String longestPalindrome(String s) {
    int n = s.length();
    boolean[][] dp = new boolean[n][n];
    int start = 0, maxLen = 1;
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) dp[i][i] = true;
    for (int len = 2; len <= n; len++) {
        for (int i = 0; i + len - 1 < n; i++) {
            int j = i + len - 1;
            if (s.charAt(i) == s.charAt(j)) {
                dp[i][j] = len == 2 || dp[i + 1][j - 1];
            } else {
                dp[i][j] = false;
            }
            if (dp[i][j] && len > maxLen) { maxLen = len; start = i; }
        }
    }
    return s.substring(start, start + maxLen);
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n²) | Space: O(n²)

        

    


    
    

        

            29. Longest Palindromic Subsequence (LC 516)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given a string s, return the length of its longest palindromic subsequence.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: s = "bbbab"  |  Output: 4  (e.g. "bbbb")

            
Test Case 2:
Input: s = "cbbd"  |  Output: 2

        

        

            
2D DP: dp[i][j] = LPS length in s[i..j] (s = "bbbab")

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            

            
Answer: ?

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int longestPalindromeSubseq(String s) {
    int n = s.length();
    int[][] dp = new int[n][n];
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) dp[i][i] = 1;
    for (int len = 2; len <= n; len++) {
        for (int i = 0; i + len - 1 < n; i++) {
            int j = i + len - 1;
            if (s.charAt(i) == s.charAt(j)) {
                dp[i][j] = 2 + dp[i + 1][j - 1];
            } else {
                dp[i][j] = Math.max(dp[i + 1][j], dp[i][j - 1]);
            }
        }
    }
    return dp[0][n - 1];
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n²) | Space: O(n²)

        

    


    
    

        

            30. Palindromic Substrings (LC 647)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given a string s, return the number of palindromic substrings in it (each distinct substring counts once).

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: s = "abc"  |  Output: 3

            
Test Case 2:
Input: s = "aaa"  |  Output: 6

        

        

            
Expand from center (s = "aab")

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            
a a b

            
center: —  |  count: 0

            
Answer: ?

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
int countSubstrings(String s) {
    int n = s.length();
    int count = 0;
    for (int c = 0; c < 2 * n - 1; c++) {
        int lo = c / 2, hi = c / 2 + c % 2;
        while (lo >= 0 && hi < n && s.charAt(lo) == s.charAt(hi)) {
            count++;
            lo--;
            hi++;
        }
    }
    return count;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n²) | Space: O(1)

        

    


    
    

        

            31. Word Break (LC 139)
            Medium
        

        

            
Problem: Given a string s and a dictionary of strings, return true if s can be segmented into a space-separated sequence of one or more dictionary words.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: s = "leetcode", wordDict = ["leet","code"]  |  Output: true

            
Test Case 2:
Input: s = "applepenapple", wordDict = ["apple","pen"]  |  Output: true

        

        

            
dp[i] = can segment s[0..i-1] (s = "leetcode", dict = leet, code)

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            
try word: —

            

                
i012345678

                
dp
                    -----
                    ----

            

            
Answer: ?

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
boolean wordBreak(String s, List wordDict) {
    Set dict = new HashSet<>(wordDict);
    int n = s.length();
    boolean[] dp = new boolean[n + 1];
    dp[0] = true;
    for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
        for (int j = 0; j < i; j++) {
            if (dp[j] && dict.contains(s.substring(j, i))) {
                dp[i] = true;
                break;
            }
        }
    }
    return dp[n];
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: O(n³) naive substring; O(n²) with hashing | Space: O(n)

        

    


    
    

        

            32. Word Break II (LC 140)
            Hard
        

        

            
Problem: Given a string s and a dictionary, return all valid space-separated sentences you can form by segmenting s entirely into dictionary words.

        

        

            
Test Case 1:
Input: s = "catsanddog", wordDict = ["cat","cats","and","sand","dog"]  |  Output: ["cats and dog","cat sand dog"]

            
Test Case 2:
Input: s = "pineapplepenapple", dict = ["apple","pen","applepen","pine","pineapple"]  |  Output: multiple sentences

        

        

            
Backtracking + memo: explore splits on "catsanddog"

            

                
                
                
                
            

            
Click Play or Step to start

            
call stack (suffix start): —

            
—

            
Sentences found: ?

        

        

            
Solution (Java-style Pseudocode):

            
List wordBreak(String s, List wordDict) {
    Set dict = new HashSet<>(wordDict);
    Map> memo = new HashMap<>();
    return dfs(s, 0, dict, memo);
}
List dfs(String s, int start, Set dict, Map> memo) {
    if (memo.containsKey(start)) return memo.get(start);
    List res = new ArrayList<>();
    if (start == s.length()) { res.add(""); return res; }
    for (int end = start + 1; end <= s.length(); end++) {
        String word = s.substring(start, end);
        if (!dict.contains(word)) continue;
        for (String tail : dfs(s, end, dict, memo)) {
            res.add(tail.isEmpty() ? word : word + " " + tail);
        }
    }
    memo.put(start, res);
    return res;
}
            

            
Complexity:
Time: Exponential in worst case (pruned by memo) | Space: O(n) recursion + memo
